topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every carefully curated achievement, every whispered anecdote about my supposed 'destiny,' was there, meticulously laid out, yet none of it was mine. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that burned in my chest. I stared at the dates, the names, the accomplishments—all a stranger’s narrative draped over my actual life. A slow, dangerous heat began to bloom in my core. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescents into my palms. "This is a forgery," I whispered, the sound raspy and brittle. I ripped the page in half, the tearing sound echoing loudly in the sterile silence of my apartment. I began pacing, a caged animal vibrating with suppressed fury, my jaw muscles locking so hard I could feel the strain in my neck. I needed answers, and I needed them now.

[story 2]

When the editor handed me the final draft, the air left my lungs in a painful rush. It wasn't just a discrepancy; it was a complete hijacking of my existence. I read the passage detailing my formative years, the descriptions of my passions—it was all polished, eloquent, and utterly false. A tremor ran through my hands, making the pen slip from my grasp. I slammed the manuscript onto the mahogany desk, the impact rattling the crystal decanter beside it. My eyes narrowed, burning with a cold, focused intensity. "How dare you?" My voice was dangerously low, stripped bare of all pleasantries. I stood up abruptly, my posture rigid, every line of my body radiating a tightly coiled tension. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, my knuckles white where I gripped the sill. The sheer audacity of the deception made my vision swim; I wanted to shatter something, anything, to make the lie bleed out.

[story 3]

I found the file tucked away in the archives, a brittle, yellowed folder that smelled faintly of dust and old secrets. As I scanned the typed entries, the realization hit me with the force of a physical blow. My life, as presented to the world, was a carefully constructed fiction, authored by someone else. A profound, unsettling stillness settled over me, quickly escalating into a furious internal storm. I dropped the folder; it landed with a dull thud that seemed disproportionately loud. My breathing became shallow, rapid, punctuated by sharp intakes of air. I started moving—a jerky, agitated motion—pacing the length of the room, my gaze darting around as if searching for the source of the deceit. "You think you can just erase me?" I spat the words out, my voice vibrating with suppressed violence. I slammed my open palm against the nearest surface, the resulting dull thud a punctuation mark to my absolute refusal to accept this fabricated reality.
